retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Fine-needle aspiration is a minimally invasive way to obtain a cell sample to confirm a diagnosis or guide treatment. Fine-needle aspiration is an alternative to more invasive methods such as incisional or excisional biopsy. This activity reviews the procedure of fine-needle aspiration and reviews the role it plays in evaluating suspicious masses, as well as highlighting the role of the interprofessional team in this procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Biopsy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: For both targeted and non-targeted biopsies, core biopsy specimens have greater sensitivity and specificity compared to fine-needle aspiration specimens. [34] Consequently, the AUA guidelines recommend multiple core biopsies over fine needle aspiration for targeted biopsy, a recommendation also supported by the EAU guidelines. There is no standard minimum amount of tissue requirement for a targeted biopsy, as some pathologists can diagnose with the yield from a single fine-needle aspiration pass. Clinicians recommending the biopsy should consult with the pathologist providing the diagnostic interpretation to discuss the desirable molecular testing, which may affect the amount of tissue needed. The pathologist, in turn, should consult with the clinician performing the procedure (if different) to specify the amount of tissue required to conduct tests for genetic markers, flow cytometry, culture, and sensitivity. The necessary sample size can vary from one pathologist/institution to another; there are no standardized guidelines for the minimum tissue volume needed in advance.

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for fine-needle aspiration. Describe the technique in regards to effectively performing fine-needle aspiration. Review the appropriate evaluation of the potential complications of performing fine-needle aspiration. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to properly perform fine-needle aspiration and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: If the fine-needle aspiration is to be performed via endoscopy or bronchoscopy, the lesion may be aspirated through direct visualization or with the addition of ultrasound guidance. If the lesion is not visible, for example, if a paratracheal lymph node needs to be sampled, then an ultrasound will be necessary. This requires a specialized scope with an ultrasound probe attached as well as a device on the tip of the scope to deploy the needle. Still, the ultimate technique is the same: inserting the needle into the area under investigation while aspirating.

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration of Breast Masses -- Clinical Significance -- Accuracy
retrieved_docs.content: Certain studies have cited the sensitivity of aspiration cytology for malignancy at 64% for one aspiration sample and 91% in patients with three samples. Specificity was 56% (inadequate or unsatisfactory cytological preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Fine-needle aspiration is also an important triage in deep-seated lesions such as in lung, mediastinum, and abdominal organs, including pancreas and lymph nodes, especially in patients who are not fit for more invasive biopsy sampling.

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: If the area to be aspirated is superficial, then simply sterilizing the area before performing fine-needle aspiration is all that is necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration of Breast Masses -- Clinical Significance -- Disadvantages
retrieved_docs.content: Lower diagnostic yield than core needle biopsy Four percent to 13% of samples found to be inadequate or nondiagnostic More difficult to distinguish invasive versus in-situ carcinoma May require more experienced cytopathologist for diagnosis Non-diagnostic results necessitate repeat biopsy (typically CNB or vacuum-assisted)

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration of Breast Masses -- Clinical Significance -- Advantages of FNA over other Modalities
retrieved_docs.content: Cost-effective (declines with inconclusive results/need for repeat biopsies) Less invasive/less painful secondary to smaller needle size Can be performed in the clinic or office Appropriate for patients who are being anticoagulated with low risk of hematoma/bleeding Can be used to sample axillary tissue

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Nodule Biopsy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Fine needle aspiration (FNA) was initially performed in the 1930s when Martin and Ellis first described the procedure and the potential implications in the medical field. [1] Initially, interest in the procedure remained low due in part to concerns with regards to cancer seeding. Walfish later described the use of combined ultrasound guidance and needle aspiration cytology in increasing the diagnostic accuracy of specimen acquisition. [2] The role of ultrasound-guided FNA has evolved over time as an important tool in the assessment of thyroid nodules. The important aspects of the FNA procedure include: Minimizing patient discomfort Maintaining patient safety Maintaining proper ultrasound and needle technique in order to visualize needle entry to the target nodule Ensuring adequate acquisition of tissue sample

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Marrow Aspiration and Biopsy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A potential source of error specifically pertinent to this procedure is the lack of adequate assistance. Utilizing assistants who are aware of the steps of the procedure, how the slides are prepared, and how the biopsy sample is prepared is key to ensuring that the procedure goes smoothly. Ensuring that an assistant is available can aid the clinician in performing the procedure so that they may focus on their own tasks. This can also be beneficial to the patient as the procedure time may decrease, and the number of sampling sites may be reduced due to the higher sampling quality. As previously mentioned, communication with the pathology department is also essential for obtaining high-quality samples.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Nodule Biopsy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Nonaspiration technique involves performing this maneuver without suction, with capillary action allowing the collection of the specimen within the needle hub. Aspiration technique allows for specimen acquisition via operator application of gentle suction during the to and fro maneuver. Although nonaspiration technique has occasionally been shown to be superior to the aspiration technique, two meta-analysis studies revealed no significant difference between the two. [18] [19] [20] The needle-syringes may be delivered to a cytopathologist present during the procedure for further processing to ensure adequate acquisition. Multiple aspiration attempts are performed until a satisfactory tissue sample is confirmed by the cytopathologist.

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Marrow Aspiration and Biopsy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the indications for a bone marrow aspiration and biopsy. Identify the equipment and personnel needed to perform a bone marrow aspiration and biopsy. Outline the potential complications of bone marrow aspirations and biopsies. Explain the importance of improving the coordination of care between members of the interprofessional care team in order to improve outcomes for patients who undergo a bone marrow aspiration and biopsy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Marrow Aspiration and Biopsy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: If a biopsy is required, the biopsy needle will next be advanced into the same skin entry site but will be directed at a slightly different angle towards the bone to avoid the previously aspirated site. The needle should be advanced with a greater amount of pressure than previously used into the bone. The needle should then be turned both clockwise and counterclockwise to loosen the sample. With a twisting motion, the needle can then be removed, and the sample can be extracted and placed into the proper container to be sent to pathology. If an insufficient sample has been obtained, a new biopsy needle should be used, and the biopsy process should be repeated.

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Marrow Aspiration and Biopsy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bone marrow aspiration and biopsies are often performed both in the inpatient and outpatient settings for the diagnosis of a multitude of hematologic disorders. Both the palpation-guided and computed tomography-guided (CT-guided) approaches are commonly utilized for performing this procedure. Both procedural methods have a high degree of accuracy and a low complication rate. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2598
retrieved_docs.content: Fine needle biopsy of the ovary is a specific application of the fine needle biopsy technique (خزعة بإبرة رفيعة), utilizing a thin needle to obtain tissue samples from ovarian lesions for diagnostic evaluation. This method allows minimally invasive access to ovarian tissue, facilitating accurate histopathological analysis while reducing procedural morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1948
retrieved_docs.content: Fine needle biopsy of ovary is a diagnostic procedure performed on female reproductive organs and is classified among other diagnostic procedures for female organs. It is synonymous with the Arabic term “خزعة بإبرة رفيعة,” indicating that this technique is the same method referred to by that phrase.

These procedures are grouped under “إجراءات هيستوباثولوجيا متعلقة بالأعضاء التناسلية والثدي” and the child category “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs,” which itself is a child of عملية للأعضاء التناسلية الأنثوية (female genital‑organ procedures). خزرعة بطانة الرحم (endometrial biopsy) is the target‑anatomy for both open and diagnostic dilation‑and‑curettage (D&C) of the uterus, illustrating a common diagnostic pathway for uterine pathology. * Aspiration curettage after delivery or abortion, classified as “Dilatation and curettage (D&C); aspiration after delivery or abortion,” also employs الخزعة for tissue retrieval. * Male genital diagnostics, specifically transrectal prostate biopsy, is a child of عمليات البروستاتا (prostate operations) and belongs to “diagnostic procedures; male genital,” highlighting its role in prostate pathology assessment. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: & Gynecology also encompasses related surgical procedures such as salpingo‑oophorectomy (complete or partial, unilateral or bilateral). The concept of “Organ” connects these procedures to their anatomical targets, linking the excised tissues (e.g., vaginal biopsy, fine‑needle ovarian biopsy) to the organs themselves and to endoscopic access methods (pelvic endoscopy, uterine hysteroscopy). This integrated hierarchy provides a systematic framework for the histopathological evaluation of gynecologic and ovarian tissues, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic approaches and ensuring accurate tissue sampling within obstetric‑gynecologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 65
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage performed after delivery or abortion is classified under dilatation and curettage (D&C) as “Aspiration curettage following delivery or abortion.” Irrigation of an implanted venous access device is also explicitly excluded from simultaneous coding with فصادة الصفيحات, laparoscopic treatment of ectopic pregnancy – without salpingectomy and/or oophorectomy, and kidney transplant, indicating mutual exclusivity in clinical documentation. The diagnostic hierarchy places “Diagnostic” at the top, encompassing “Diagnostic procedures on ovaries” and “Diagnostic dilation and curettage of uterus,” both belonging to “other diagnostic procedures; female organs” and to the broader group of diagnostic services for the female genital system. Fine‑needle biopsy of the ovary (خزعة بإبرة رفيعة) is synonymous with “other diagnostic procedures; female organs,” listed as an excised‑anatomy procedure under the generic entity Organ, which also includes vaginal biopsy. Open uterine biopsy

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: Biospecimen Collection Method includes the procedure known as الخزعة (biopsy) as a specific technique for obtaining tissue samples, and diagnostic endocrine procedures are broadly associated with الخزعة, reflecting the use of biopsy in the evaluation of endocrine disorders. This relationship highlights the central role of biopsy both as a method of biospecimen collection and as a key component of diagnostic strategies in endocrine medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 699
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on ovaries and diagnostic dilation and curettage of uterus are specific diagnostic interventions that belong to the broader group of other diagnostic procedures; female organs. Both procedures are classified as diagnostic procedures for the female genital system, indicating that they are used to evaluate ovarian and uterine pathology respectively. The general concept “Diagnostic” is intended to encompass these ovarian and uterine diagnostic procedures. Fine needle biopsy of ovary is considered equivalent to the category of other diagnostic procedures; female organs and is performed using the method described in Arabic as “خزعة بإبرة رفيعة” (fine‑needle biopsy). Together, these entities illustrate the hierarchical organization of female‑specific diagnostic techniques, linking individual procedures to their parent category and specifying the method employed for fine‑needle ovarian sampling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1579
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial sampling (biopsy) with or without endocervical sampling (biopsy), without cervical dilation, any method (separate procedure) is a diagnostic procedure that falls within the specialty of physicians practicing obstetrics and gynecology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 644
retrieved_docs.content: Endocervical biopsy, other diagnostic procedures on the cervix, and fine‑needle biopsy of the ovary are all classified under the broader category of “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs.” This category also includes vaginal biopsy (خزعة المهبل), uterine hysteroscopy (منظار الرحم), and pelvic‑cavity endoscopy (تنظير جوف الحوض). Endocervical biopsy is a procedure that excises cervical tissue, while fine‑needle biopsy of the ovary employs a thin‑needle technique (خزعة بإبرة رفيعة) to obtain ovarian samples. Both vaginal biopsy and fine‑needle ovarian biopsy are identified as excisional procedures (procedure_has_excised_anatomy), whereas pelvic‑cavity endoscopy and uterine hysteroscopy are identified as target‑anatomy procedures (procedure_has_target_anatomy) that visualize internal female pelvic structures. Collectively, these interrelated diagnostic methods provide clinicians with essential tissue samples and visual assessments for evaluating a wide range of gynecologic conditions, thereby supporting accurate diagnosis and informed patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2772
retrieved_docs.content: Physicians specializing in Obstetrics & Gynecology are the qualified providers for performing both ovarian biopsy (خزعة المبيض) and endometrial sampling (biopsy) with or without endocervical sampling, performed without cervical dilation and using any method as a separate procedure. These procedures fall within the scope of Obstetrics & Gynecology practice, highlighting the specialty’s role in obtaining tissue specimens from the ovary, endometrium, and endocervix for diagnostic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 217
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base defines “biopsy of the female genital system” as a broad diagnostic category that encompasses both ovarian biopsy (خزعة المبيض) and uterine biopsy (خزعة الرحم). Obstetrics & Gynecology physicians are identified as the clinical specialists who perform these procedures. All of these interventions are classified under the hierarchical group “other diagnostic procedures; female organs,” which is a subcategory of “إجراءات هيستوباثولوجيا متعلقة بالأعضاء التناسلية والثدي” (histopathology procedures related to genital and breast organs). Within the “other diagnostic procedures; female organs” group, specific techniques are organized as follows: - Closed biopsy of the uterus, recorded in Arabic as endometrial biopsy (خزعة بطانة الرحم), and open biopsy of the uterus, both aimed at sampling the uterine lining. - Endocervical biopsy, a diagnostic excision of tissue from the cervical canal. - Other diagnostic procedures on the cervix. - Fine‑needle (thin‑needle)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2808
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage following delivery or abortion is classified as a form of dilatation and curettage (D&C) performed as an aspiration procedure after delivery or abortion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage performed after delivery or abortion is classified as a dilatation‑and‑curettage (D&C) procedure within the “procedure of the female genital organs” hierarchy and is linked to the diagnostic intent “Diagnostic” as an inverse‑isa specialization of a general diagnostic procedure for the female genital system. Diagnostic biopsies of the female reproductive system—including ovarian biopsy (خزعة المبيض), fine‑needle ovarian biopsy (خزعة بإبرة رفيعة), uterine biopsy (خزعة الرحم), closed and open uterine biopsies, endometrial biopsy (خزعة بطانة الرحم), endocervical biopsy, and vaginal biopsy (خزعة المهبل)—are grouped under the subcategory “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs” and are associated with pelvic endoscopy (تنظير جوف الحوض) and hysteroscopy (منظار الرحم). Endometrial biopsy (خزعة بطانة الرحم) is a specific form of uterine biopsy (خزعة الرحم) via an inverse‑isa relationship and serves as a parent procedure in the histopathology group “إجراءات

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2708
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic endocrine procedures represent a broad category of diagnostic techniques that include الخزعة (biopsy). Within the scope of الخزعة, there are more specific child procedures, such as لطاخة المهبل (vaginal swab), which is a specialized form of biopsy targeting the vaginal mucosa. Recognizing that diagnostic endocrine procedures encompass الخزعة and that الخزعة has لطاخة المهبل as a sub‑procedure helps clinicians understand the hierarchical organization of these diagnostic methods and select the most appropriate technique for endocrine assessments and vaginal tissue sampling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 99
retrieved_docs.content: The community defines a comprehensive taxonomy for gynecologic and related surgical procedures. Biopsy of the female genital system comprises uterine biopsy (خزعة الرحم) and ovarian biopsy (خزعة المبيض), classified under histopathology procedures related to genital and breast organs (إجراءات هيستوباثولوجيا متعلقة بالأعضاء التناسلية والثدي) and performed by obstetrics‑gynecology physicians. Diagnostic biopsies such as closed biopsy of the uterus, open biopsy of the uterus, endocervical biopsy, vaginal biopsy (خزعة المهبل), fine‑needle ovarian biopsy (خزعة بإبرة رفيعة), and other diagnostic procedures on the cervix are grouped in the category “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs,” which is linked to pelvic endoscopy (تنظير جوف الحوض) and uterine hysteroscopy (منظار الرحم). Endometrial sampling (خزعة بطانة الرحم) may be performed with or without endocervical sampling and is related to diagnostic dilatation and curettage (D&C). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 181
retrieved_docs.content: is an organ. Organ is defined as encompassing gonads (gonády) and lymph nodes (lymphfatické uzliny) and is the target of pelvic cavity endoscopy (تنظير جوف الحوض). Organ is also the source of excised anatomies such as vaginal biopsy, thin‑needle biopsy, and endometrial biopsy. This integrated hierarchy clarifies how uterine, ovarian, cervical, and vaginal biopsy techniques, as well as fine‑needle and dilation‑and‑curettage methods, are organized within the overall framework of female‑organ diagnostic and histopathological procedures, supporting clinicians in mapping sampling methods to specific organ systems and pathology workflows.
